title: Sinews of War at Sea: The Armed Services Need a Common Watercraft Family
gist: The US joint force is proliferating incompatible intra-theater watercraft programs with collapsing readiness; the authors argue Congress should force consolidation around the commercial Damen landing-ship family (already chosen by the Navy/Marines as the McClung-class) to gain scale, interoperability, and wartime replaceability.

insights.claim: The Army's Maneuver Support Vessel (Light) will cost $63.1 million apiece for a $1 billion program of only 13 hulls — cut from 36 — each hauling 82 tons, roughly one M1 Abrams tank with little room for fuel, ammunition, or troops.
insights.speaker: Peter Mitchell and John Curl
insights.so_what: A tiny, expensive fleet illustrates the cost risk of bespoke military watercraft design.

insights.claim: The Navy and Marine Corps abandoned their bespoke medium landing ship in late 2024 after industry bids far overran budget, and in December 2025 selected the commercial Damen Landing Ship Transport-100, designated the McClung-class with a plan of 18–35 hulls.
insights.speaker: Peter Mitchell and John Curl
insights.so_what: Marks a decisive shift toward commercial-off-the-shelf hulls after bespoke shipbuilding failed on cost.
insights.locator: The Navy and Marine Corps spent years

insights.claim: The Army is separately developing the Maneuver Support Vessel (Heavy) to replace its 4,199-ton Besson-class Logistics Support Vessels aging out between 2028 and 2038, with no design chosen, no firm production numbers, and no fielding timeline.
insights.speaker: Peter Mitchell and John Curl
insights.so_what: An open, undefined heavy-watercraft program is the target the authors want redirected to Damen.

insights.claim: The Damen design the Navy selected is the same commercial hull Australia chose for its eight-ship Landing Craft Heavy program and the Nigerian Navy also operates, meaning forward repair and logistics support already exist in-theater.
insights.speaker: Peter Mitchell and John Curl
insights.so_what: Allied fleet commonality is the strongest under-priced argument for interoperability and sustainment.

insights.claim: A 120-meter Damen variant, made by adding a 20-meter modular block, is reported to carry 1,500 square meters of roll-on/roll-off cargo, 80 vehicles, and 400 troops at 14.5 knots — close to the Army's stated heavy requirement of 400 feet, 18 knots, and 175 soldiers.
insights.speaker: Peter Mitchell and John Curl
insights.so_what: The speed shortfall is the only open question a formal hull-family review would need to resolve.
insights.locator: The stated requirements for the Army’s heavy watercraft

insights.claim: Damen-based medium landing ship cost estimates vary widely — Navy figures near $155 million per hull at scale versus the Congressional Budget Office's $350–430 million — so 25 ships would run about $3.9 billion at the low end to $8.5–10.7 billion at the high end.
insights.speaker: Peter Mitchell and John Curl
insights.so_what: Even at the high estimate a shared commercial hull may beat a separate Army design that has no published cost.

insights.claim: A 2023 Army Futures Command analysis rejected the Navy's vessel as too small and slow, but that assessment predated the Navy's late-2025 switch to the commercial Damen design and is therefore outdated.
insights.speaker: Peter Mitchell and John Curl
insights.so_what: The institutional case against a common hull rests on a stale comparison — grounds for a fresh review.

insights.claim: The Army watercraft fleet's mission-capable rate fell from about 75 percent in 2020 to 35 percent in 2024, with Logistics Support Vessels and Utility Landing Craft averaging more than three decades old and one vessel out of service for over five years.
insights.speaker: Peter Mitchell and John Curl
insights.so_what: The existing sealift fleet is collapsing, adding urgency to any recapitalization decision.
insights.locator: During the same period, the Army watercraft

insights.claim: The Army's light watercraft prototype broke down en route to Camp Pendleton in early 2024, was towed to Newport News, and spent nearly a year in repair including a $200,000 cooling-system fix because the Army lacks global maintenance support for its watercraft.
insights.speaker: Peter Mitchell and John Curl
insights.so_what: New hull types are being added faster than the maintenance architecture to keep them running.
insights.locator: The Army’s latest light maneuver support vessel

themes.theme: Scale over exquisiteness (the Liberty ship lesson)

insights.claim: The US built 49,234 Sherman tanks between 1942 and 1945 versus Germany's roughly 6,000 technically superior Panthers, and Germany's edge did not matter because it could not produce, crew, maintain, or replace at the rate the war consumed them.
insights.speaker: Peter Mitchell and John Curl
insights.so_what: Core thesis: in attrition warfare, scale and standardization beat platform exquisiteness.

insights.claim: Between 1941 and 1945, 2,710 Liberty ships were built across 18 shipyards — an average of three every two days — using a commercial design, showing pre-existing designs let industrial capacity surge into wartime output.
insights.speaker: Peter Mitchell and John Curl
insights.so_what: Historical anchor for why commercial-derived hulls broaden the shipyard base under wartime demand.

insights.claim: In a protracted high-attrition peer conflict, production and interoperability are more decisive than exquisiteness, and the joint force already lacks sufficient sustainment capacity as shown by Joint Logistics Over-the-Shore in Gaza and the wars in Ukraine and Iran.
insights.speaker: Peter Mitchell and John Curl
insights.so_what: Frames sustainment shipbuilding as the joint force's most important acquisition problem this decade.
insights.locator: In a protracted high attrition peer conflict

insights.claim: There is no joint acquisition integrator for intra-theater watercraft, and US Transportation Command — the logical integrator — has no authority or resourcing, so each service buys to its own concept of operations and will compete for the same sea lanes, repair capacity, and ports in a contingency.
insights.speaker: Peter Mitchell and John Curl
insights.so_what: Identifies the governance gap Congress would need to close to force consolidation.
insights.locator: There is no joint acquisition integrator

insights.claim: Congress warned in June 2026 that the Army may not provide resourcing consistent with a realistic strategy to support the watercraft needed for Pacific operations.
insights.speaker: Peter Mitchell and John Curl
insights.so_what: Congressional attention is the lever the authors expect to drive the consolidation they recommend.
insights.quote: the Army may not provide resourcing consistent with a realistic strategy to support the watercraft needed for operations in the Pacific

insights.claim: The authors recommend Congress designate Transportation Command as lead integrator at Fort Eustis, condition Maneuver Support Vessel (Heavy) funding on a hull-family review against the 100- and 120-meter Damen variants, require a joint crewing pipeline, and mandate a single enterprise readiness reporting system.
insights.speaker: Peter Mitchell and John Curl
insights.so_what: Concrete legislative path that, if enacted, would consolidate demand onto the Damen family.

insights.claim: The F-35 cautionary analogy does not apply because a landing ship, unlike a fifth-generation fighter, has no physical constraint like the F-35B's vertical landing forcing incompatible variants, so a shared Army-Marine watercraft family is achievable.
insights.speaker: Peter Mitchell and John Curl
insights.so_what: Pre-empts the standard objection that joint programs always produce compromised platforms.
insights.locator: Writing this way of a modular joint acquisition program
